title: How to Fix phpMyAdmin Import Timeout (The Complete Guide)
description: Learn why phpMyAdmin times out and 4 proven methods to fix it, including splitting large SQL files.
author: SQLSplit Team

content.text: If you've ever tried importing a large SQL file into phpMyAdmin, you've probably hit the dreaded timeout error. This guide covers why it happens and 4 proven methods to fix it — from quick configuration tweaks to splitting your database into manageable chunks.
Why phpMyAdmin Times Out (The Real Reasons)
phpMyAdmin is a web application written in PHP, and like all PHP applications, it's constrained by server configuration limits. When you import a large SQL file, three specific limits come into play:

items: **max_execution_time** — How long a PHP script can run (default: 30 seconds)
**memory_limit** — How much RAM PHP can use (default: 128MB)

content.text: When your import hits any of these limits, phpMyAdmin stops dead. The frustrating part? You might see different error messages depending on which limit you hit:

items: "The uploaded file exceeds the upload_max_filesize directive"
Or simply: a blank white screen

content.text: Method 1: Increase PHP Limits (The Quick Fix)
The fastest solution is to increase PHP's limits. This works for moderately large files (up to ~100MB), but shared hosting providers often block these changes.
For cPanel Users

items: Find "Select PHP Version" or "MultiPHP INI Editor"

Increase these values:
   - max_execution_time = 300
   - max_input_time = 300
   - memory_limit = 512M
   - post_max_size = 512M
   - upload_max_filesize = 512M

content.text: For XAMPP/MAMP Users
Edit your php.ini file:

# Windows: C:\xampp\php\php.ini
# Mac: /Applications/MAMP/bin/php/php8.x.x/conf/php.ini

max_execution_time = 300
max_input_time = 300
memory_limit = 512M
post_max_size = 512M
upload_max_filesize = 512M
Then restart Apache.

**Note:** On shared hosting, you may not have access to php.ini. Try creating a php.ini or .user.ini file in your public_html folder with these values. If that doesn't work, contact your host or use Method 2 below.
Method 2: Split Your SQL File (RECOMMENDED)

For files over 100MB — or when you can't change PHP limits — splitting your SQL file is the most reliable solution. Instead of one massive import, you get several smaller files that import quickly and reliably.
How to Split Your SQL File

items:
Choose your split method:
   - **By Lines:** 10,000 lines per file (good for most cases)
   - **By Size:** 10MB per file (matches phpMyAdmin limits)
Import each file into phpMyAdmin, one at a time

content.text: Why This Works

items: Each small file imports in seconds, not minutes
If one chunk fails, you don't lose everything
Works on any host, even restrictive shared hosting

content.text: **Privacy Note:** SQLSplit runs entirely in your browser. Your database never leaves your computer — we don't have a server that processes your files. This is crucial when working with sensitive data.
Method 3: Use the MySQL Command Line
If you have SSH access to your server, the command line is the fastest and most reliable method. No file size limits, no timeouts.
Import via Command Line
mysql -u username -p database_name < your_file.sql
You'll be prompted for your password. Then wait — large imports can take minutes or hours, but they won't time out.
For Compressed Files

# For .gz files
gunzip < your_file.sql.gz | mysql -u username -p database_name

# For .zip files
unzip -p your_file.sql.zip | mysql -u username -p database_name
Pros and Cons
Method 4: BigDump.php (Not Recommended)
You might find tutorials recommending BigDump.php — a PHP script that imports large SQL files in chunks. While it works, we don't recommend it for security reasons:

items: Requires uploading the script to your server

content.text: Use one of the other three methods instead.
FAQ
What size is too big for phpMyAdmin?
It depends on your server configuration, but generally:

items: **50-500MB:** Split the file or use command line

content.text: Will splitting my SQL file break my database?

No — if you use a proper SQL splitter. SQLSplit preserves your database structure, including headers (SET statements, character sets) and footers. Each chunk is a valid SQL file that can be imported independently.
How long should a database import take?
Rough estimates:
Factors affecting speed: server CPU, disk I/O, network speed, number of indexes, and INSERT statement complexity.
Can I resume a failed import?
Not easily with phpMyAdmin. That's why splitting is recommended — if chunk 5 of 10 fails, you only need to re-import that one chunk, not start over.
Summary: Which Method Should You Choose?

content.title: Ready to fix your import?
content.text: Try SQLSplit free — no signup required, works in your browser, handles files up to 2GB.

faq.question: What size is too big for phpMyAdmin?
faq.answer: Under 10MB usually works fine. 10-50MB may need PHP limit increases. 50-500MB should be split or use command line. Over 500MB strongly recommends command line.

faq.question: Will splitting my SQL file break my database?
faq.answer: No — if you use a proper SQL splitter. SQLSplit preserves your database structure, including headers and footers. Each chunk is a valid SQL file.

faq.question: How long should a database import take?
faq.answer: 10MB: 30 seconds - 2 minutes. 100MB: 2-10 minutes. 1GB: 10-60 minutes. 10GB: 1-6 hours. Speed depends on server CPU, disk I/O, and network.

faq.question: Can I resume a failed import?
faq.answer: Not easily with phpMyAdmin. That is why splitting is recommended — if chunk 5 of 10 fails, you only need to re-import that one chunk.
